description: Stage 3.2: bounded state-conditioned controllers over the executable strong 8192-tokenizer local base. All active slots are training-side controller policies on the same concrete trunk.

sanity.description: Crash check and early gate-behavior screen for controller slots.

screen.description: Short controller ranking on deployed score and step cost.

decision.description: Long single-GPU runs for promoted controller finalists.

champion_8x.description: Full-box confirmation run for the best controller.

slots.name: static_control_a
slots.why: Primary static control. Strong static base with no dynamic controller.
slots.validates: The copied strong base is stable and provides the correct reference for controller deltas.
slots.falsifies: All controller conclusions are suspect if the control itself is unstable.

slots.name: static_control_b
slots.why: Noise-floor control with a different seed.
slots.validates: Short-horizon variance is low enough to promote controller winners confidently.
slots.falsifies: If control spread is large, controller deltas in sanity and screen are not trustworthy.

slots.name: late_deploy_gate
slots.broken_invariant: One deploy-alignment law should apply for the whole run.
slots.mechanism: Keep early and mid training mostly static, then activate a late export-surrogate plus soft QAT ramp and denser late snapshot capture.
slots.why: Deploy-facing pressure is likely harmful early and valuable late. The controller should internalize deploy alignment only after the representation is mostly formed.
slots.early_signal: Late-phase logs should show nonzero qat_alpha and snapshot capture without a large step-time collapse. post_quant_bpb should beat R0A even if pre_quant is flat.
slots.failure_mode: Late deploy pressure still arrives too early or is too weak to matter. It may also shrink raw fit more than it shrinks quant damage.
slots.kill_rule: No post_quant_bpb improvement vs R0A by screen, or step_avg_ms regresses by >12% without a score win.
slots.validates: Late gated deploy alignment beats the static default.
slots.falsifies: Static late-QAT behavior is already sufficient and controller timing does not matter.
notes: Primary question: does timing deploy pressure help more than static late-QAT alone?

slots.name: best_state_deployed
slots.broken_invariant: The last checkpoint is the correct export target.
slots.mechanism: Capture late snapshots and choose the best deployed-state candidate from the last-k states instead of always exporting the last state.
slots.why: The best compressed artifact often does not coincide with the latest raw checkpoint. This controller attacks train-to-deploy mismatch directly.
slots.early_signal: Snapshot scoring logs should show a chosen checkpoint different from raw_final or ema_final. Screen may be neutral; decision should be stronger.
slots.failure_mode: The late-state window is too narrow, or the best deployed state still coincides with EMA or the final step.
slots.kill_rule: By decision, chosen snapshot is always final/EMA and post_quant_bpb does not beat R0A.
slots.validates: Export-state selection is a real first-order mechanism.
slots.falsifies: The static end state is already the best deployable state.

slots.name: best_state_plus_ttt_last2
slots.broken_invariant: The best pre-quant TTT start state is the final EMA checkpoint and the same freeze depth should always be used.
slots.mechanism: Select the best deployed late state, then run pre-quant TTT on that state while freezing the first 9 blocks and adapting the last 2 with a uniform LR.
slots.why: The current frontier treats pre-quant TTT as a major downstream stage. This controller asks whether deployed checkpoint choice plus a narrow last-2-block TTT finisher is already a real gain on top of the strong static base.
slots.early_signal: Snapshot selection logs should choose a concrete late state and TTT logs should show the last-2-only finisher lowering chunk loss without a large step-time collapse.
slots.failure_mode: The chosen state does not matter because TTT washes it out, or last-2-only adaptation is too narrow to buy a deployed win.
slots.kill_rule: No post_quant_bpb gain vs H202 by decision, or TTT logs show little adaptation benefit while adding clear runtime overhead.
slots.validates: Checkpoint selection and pre-quant TTT interact constructively, even with a narrow freeze-depth choice.
slots.falsifies: Best-state selection is not a meaningful TTT precursor, or narrow TTT is too weak on this base.
notes: Narrow pre-quant TTT with last-2-block adaptation.

slots.name: family_split_warmdown
slots.broken_invariant: All parameter families should follow the same late adaptation law.
slots.mechanism: During warmdown, freeze token updates, damp head/scalar movement, and let matrix trunk updates continue slightly more aggressively.
slots.why: Embeddings, head, scalars, and trunk matrices likely do not want the same late dynamics. This controller changes the late basin shape instead of changing the whole run.
slots.early_signal: Step cost should stay neutral while late deployed score improves. Raw loss may move little or even worsen slightly.
slots.failure_mode: Selective freezing hurts fit more than it helps deploy robustness.
slots.kill_rule: No post_quant gain by decision, or clear raw-collapse in screen with no deploy compensation.
slots.validates: Parameter-family splitting matters in the late phase.
slots.falsifies: Uniform late adaptation is already near-optimal for this stack.
notes: This is a late-basin-shaping hypothesis, not an early-learning-speed hypothesis.

slots.name: alternating_objective
slots.broken_invariant: One blended objective should be applied every step.
slots.mechanism: Keep the main loss clean on most steps, then inject sparse late export-surrogate pulses instead of a globally blended deploy term.
slots.why: If deploy alignment is helpful late but poisonous when applied continuously, sparse pulses should recover the benefit at lower throughput cost.
slots.early_signal: Pulse logs should appear late, step cost should remain close to control, and post_quant should improve more than pre_quant.
slots.failure_mode: Pulses are either too weak to matter or too expensive relative to the step budget.
slots.kill_rule: No post_quant win by screen, or step_avg_ms regresses by >10% without deployed gain.
slots.validates: Alternating late objective pressure beats static always-on pressure.
slots.falsifies: Sparse pulses are too weak; only continuous objective shaping works.
notes: Uses pulses rather than a permanent secondary objective.

slots.name: best_state_plus_dttt_tail
slots.broken_invariant: Pre-quant TTT should use a single uniform finisher law across all unfrozen late blocks.
slots.mechanism: Select the best deployed late state, then run a broader dTTT-style finisher: freeze only the first 2 blocks, adapt the upper trunk with block-wise LR decay, and choose from the resulting deployed state.
slots.why: The latest frontier no longer says just ‘do TTT’. It says the law of TTT matters. This slot tests whether a broader discriminative tail beats the narrow last-2-only variant.
slots.early_signal: TTT logs should show progressively lower chunk loss across epochs and the broader-tail finisher should beat H207 or H202 on deployed score.
slots.failure_mode: Broader adaptation overfits, becomes too expensive, or the discriminative tail adds no value beyond the simpler last-2 finisher.
slots.kill_rule: No deployed-score gain vs H207 by decision, or the broader-tail TTT shows clear instability without a compensating post_quant win.
slots.validates: The TTT law itself is a first-order process decision worth controlling, not just the late snapshot choice.
slots.falsifies: A broader dTTT-style tail does not beat narrower last-block adaptation on this stack.
notes: Broader discriminative tail over the upper trunk.
